title: Google I/O 2025 – KbTJNK1ALYc
text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] PATRICK FUENTES:
Hi, I'm Patrick. DERECK BRIDIE: And I'm Dereck. PATRICK FUENTES: And together,
we're here to talk to you about ways you can take your 2D
Android apps to the next level in XR with 3D content. For those of you who
aren't already familiar, Android XR is the newest
platform in the Android family. It's built for extended
reality devices like headsets and glasses. The first Android XR
device will be a headset, Project Moohan from Samsung. Starting from its launch
later this year, the ecosystem will expand further across
headsets and glasses. In today's session, we'll cover
some of the 3D capabilities introduced in the developer
preview of the Jetpack XR SDK, and some practical ways you can
use them to make the content in your app really
stand out on XR devices. To do this, we'll use a 2D
app for learning about animals as an example. Throughout this talk, we'll
add 3D capabilities to the app to help that tiger and the
other animals come to life. This will take advantage of the
XR environment and create a more immersive, engaging user
experience than what's possible in 2D. We've made the new
Android XR emulator available as a part of
the developer preview, so you can try out the features
we'll cover today for yourself. One of the great things
about Android XR, is that it works hard to support
apps with little or no changes. By default, apps that
don't make changes for XR, will run in a mode
called Home Space. Home Space is the
first thing users see when they put on a headset. It's kind of like
freeform desktop Windows for your Android app
but on a seemingly infinite virtual display. Android apps without
XR affordances, open in a single
panel in Home Space. One of the major
benefits of Home Space, is that it enables side-by-side
multitasking with other apps. Home Space is great, but it's
just a 2D surface for your app inside a 3D space. To access the full
capabilities of the 3D space, you need to access a different
mode called Full Space.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – KbTJNK1ALYc
text:
is that it enables side-by-side
multitasking with other apps. Home Space is great, but it's
just a 2D surface for your app inside a 3D space. To access the full
capabilities of the 3D space, you need to access a different
mode called Full Space. Full Space is the setting
where your app has full access to the immersive
capabilities of Android XR, including 3D content. When an app enters Full
Space, other apps are hidden and the app comes into focus,
taking up the full view around you. To help your app transition
between Home Space and Full Space, we provide
APIs that can switch back and forth at runtime. To learn more,
check out our guide on transitioning from
Home Space to Full Space on
developer.android.com, or see how this is implemented in the
Hello, Android XR sample app. So now that both Home
Space and Full Space, it might be tempting
to only support Full Space for the most
immersive experience your app can provide
in Android XR, but it's important
to give your users the best possible experience
for your app in both modes. This is because your
users will often want to multitask
while using your app, and they'll need your app to
run in Home Space to do that. Optimizing for Home
Space is largely the same as preparing your
app for tablets and other large-screen devices. We have another talk
on this at Google I/O, so please check out
Adaptive Android development makes your app shine
across devices. Once your app is
in Full Space mode, you can start using the
SceneCore APIs in the Jetpack XR SDK to take advantage of the
spatial capabilities of Android XR. This includes the ability
to create the right setting for the user, whether
that's keeping users grounded in the real
world with passthrough, or a virtual environment. Maybe your app wants
to transport users to a landscape far in
the past or future, or maybe you want to provide
a calming environment where your users can focus
on your app's content. Virtual environments can
take your users there. In our example app for
learning about animals,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – KbTJNK1ALYc
text:
to a landscape far in
the past or future, or maybe you want to provide
a calming environment where your users can focus
on your app's content. Virtual environments can
take your users there. In our example app for
learning about animals, we think it'd be
cool to transport our users to a peaceful
wilderness environment that matches our theme. To do that, we need to
understand some core concepts, Skybox and Geometry. You can think of a Skybox
as a distant image, like a photosphere that
surrounds your users. This creates the illusion
of a distant background such as the sky,
mountains, or a cityscape. Your Skybox can contain
image-based lighting information to determine the way Android
XR should light the 3D content around your user. Geometry, on the other
hand, adds shape and depth to your user's environment,
creating real parallax effects for a more realistic
sense of immersion. Geometry is typically
used to show the ground the user is standing
on and objects near the user. Once you're familiar
with the concepts, you'll need the right
files for your environment, and you'll need to ensure
that they're optimized to be used on Android XR. You can learn more
about environment assets and how to optimize them
in our documentation on the link on the screen. Once you have the right files,
you can load them in your app and use them to set the
spatial environment. Once that's done, our
users are immersed in our peaceful
wilderness environment. If you want to return the user
to the real world around them, you can do so by setting the
Passthrough Opacity Preference. Setting the environment
around the user is great, but let's look at some other
ways to immerse your users. Video can be an effective way
to engage users in your app on other form factors,
and Android XR introduces new ways to
take your video experience to the next level. You can wrap video around
the front of your user with a 180-degree video, or
completely surround your user in a sphere with a
360-degree video.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – KbTJNK1ALYc
text:
introduces new ways to
take your video experience to the next level. You can wrap video around
the front of your user with a 180-degree video, or
completely surround your user in a sphere with a
360-degree video. Whether your video is on
a sphere, a flat surface, or something in between you
can introduce real depth to your video by using
stereoscopic content. Stereoscopic content provides
a 3D effect by showing slightly different images to each eye. In each eye's image, the closer
an object is to the viewer, the more it's offset, mimicking
natural depth perception. The two images are normally
stored in the same frame, either above and below each
other, or side by side, like you can see here. Both approaches
are often referred to as side-by-side
formatted video, but we're also including support
for the HEVC video format, as well. Beyond delighting your
users, immersive video can serve practical purposes,
like giving your users a tour of a new physical
space in a way that's intuitive to navigate
by looking around. All of this from the safety
and comfort of their own home. The APIs that allow you to
create objects in 3D space, are provided by the
SceneCore library, and every element you create is
called an Entity in SceneCore. The Entity we're
interested in for showing the user's immersive video,
is called SurfaceEntity. As its name suggests,
it creates a surface that we can render video to. While it's tempting to
have a stereoscopic video of a tiger leaping
towards our users, let's look at how we can use
this SurfaceEntity to wrap a 360 video of a relaxing nature
preserve around our users instead. To work with entities, and a
lot of the other capabilities of SceneCore, we'll use
the XR session class. The session is the
primary interface for apps to access
SceneCore functionality, so you'll be seeing a
lot more of it today. To create the
SurfaceEntity, we'll need to pass our instance of the
session into the factory method, and we'll specify the
format of the video, as well as the
shape of the Canvas.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – KbTJNK1ALYc
text:
so you'll be seeing a
lot more of it today. To create the
SurfaceEntity, we'll need to pass our instance of the
session into the factory method, and we'll specify the
format of the video, as well as the
shape of the Canvas. We specify mono because this
video is monoscopic, meaning it presents the same video to both
eyes, lacking depth information. We also specify that we'll
need a sphere for our Canvas, since it's a 360-degree video. We're also passing a
head pose into the method to position the sphere correctly
where the user is looking, and Dereck will share a bit
more about that in a moment. After the SurfaceEntity
is created, we can use familiar Media3
APIs, like, ExoPlayer to play the media by
setting the SurfaceEntity as the surface for your player. Once the video is
playing, our users can look around as the video
wraps around them, regardless of whether they're immersed
in a virtual environment or their real world surroundings
with passthrough enabled. Pretty cool, right. DERECK BRIDIE: Dereck here,
with some fundamental concepts for working in 3D space. When you build for Android
XR, you're quite literally adding a whole new
dimension to your app, so I wanted to make sure
that you have the tools you need to build with confidence. Earlier, we passed a head
pose to the SurfaceEntity when we created it. Poses are used to position
entities in 3D space. A pose represents the position
and orientation of an entity in 3D space, and it contains
two pieces of information, a translation and a rotation. To break this down, the
translation part is a 3D vector that allows us to specify
how far in the x, y, and z dimensions we should
move the entity to get it in the right position. When we have the entity move
to the right point in space, we use a quaternion
to specify how it should be rotating
such that it's facing the correct direction. You can copy a
rotation from one post to another to have
objects face the same way. In situations where you want
to apply a unique rotation
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – KbTJNK1ALYc
text:
it should be rotating
such that it's facing the correct direction. You can copy a
rotation from one post to another to have
objects face the same way. In situations where you want
to apply a unique rotation to an entity, you probably
want to create the quaternion from the Euler angle values,
because those are a lot easier to reason about. Euler angles
describe the rotation of an object along the three
axes colored red, green, and blue in this example. Then we can create a
quaternion to use it in a pose, using FromEulerAngles. Another important concept is
parent-child relationships. Just like in 2D layouts where
items can have children that are positioned relative to
their parents in a ViewTree, entities can have
children in a 3D scene. The position and rotation
we just talked about are always relative to
the parents of the entity. Entities can also have
components attached to them. Components are used to
add behaviors to entities. MovableComponent adds
simple controls that allow users to move the entity. ResizableComponent
adds grabbable corners that allow users to
resize the entity. There's also
InteractableComponent, which lets you capture raw input
events performed on the entity. In this example, we use
an InteractableComponent and capture the ACTION_DOWN
event to make a tiger roar. Wait, to make a tiger roar? It's time to talk about showing
your users one of the most engaging capabilities
available in Android XR, displaying 3D objects. In our example app, we
want to show the user a closeup look at a tiger. Let's take a look at how
we can make that happen. Before we get started, we'll
need an asset for the 3D model. For Android XR, we'll
want to use a glTF file. glTF support many of the
features that you would expect to see from a modern
3D-file format, including physically-based
rendering materials that can make your models look real
and add animations to bring your models to life. The simplest way to show
a glTF to your users, is by using our intent-based
3D model viewer, sceneviewerxr.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – KbTJNK1ALYc
text:
including physically-based
rendering materials that can make your models look real
and add animations to bring your models to life. The simplest way to show
a glTF to your users, is by using our intent-based
3D model viewer, sceneviewerxr. This launches the system viewer
and our app steps back to let the 3D model take center stage. When you want the user to see
the 3D model at the same time of the rest of your app's UI,
you can load a glTF resource directly in your app. This gives you more control over
things like position of the 3D model, the relative size,
or fine-grained animations. To load a 3D model
in our app, first, we'll create the
model from the glTF. We'll use a GLB file, which
is the binary transmission format for glTFs. It's optimized for smaller file
sizes and quicker loading times. Once we have the
model loaded, we'll use that model to
create an entity, and it will be
displayed to our users and lit by their environment. We can position this
entity with a pose and by setting its parent the
same way we discussed before. It looks a little
static right now. Let's bring it to life. When the model is being
displayed to our users, we can play one
of the animations contained in the model
by specifying its name and whether we'd like the
animation to loop or not. Right now, some of you may
be wondering, where should I get glTFs to use in my app? There are a variety of tools
available to create and animate glTFs. My personal favorite
being Blender. Our tiger animation
was pretty fun, but there's a lot more can do
with 3D objects in your app. Maybe your users want to see
what a sofa your company offers would look like in
their living room? Or maybe you'd like to transport
your users to Paris to see the Eiffel Tower up close? To learn more, check out our
documentation on adding 3D models to your app. If you're familiar with
ARCore mobile devices, you'll be happy to know that
we're bringing familiar concepts to Android XR, as well. ARCore for Jetpack
XR, helps your app
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – KbTJNK1ALYc
text:
To learn more, check out our
documentation on adding 3D models to your app. If you're familiar with
ARCore mobile devices, you'll be happy to know that
we're bringing familiar concepts to Android XR, as well. ARCore for Jetpack
XR, helps your app understand the real world
around the user, making use of perception capabilities. One of my favorite
features from that library is persistent anchors. To break that down, anchors
ensure that objects appear to stay at the same position
and orientation in space, helping you maintain the
illusion of virtual objects placed in the real world. Persistent anchors
is a feature that remembers the
location of a given anchor in persistent storage. To show you how
this works, we'll add one more feature
in our example app to allow users to
hang up portraits of their favorite animals in XR. First, we create an anchor. In this case, we use the
pose that our paintingEntity is currently at. Then we persist the anchor. This saves the pose
in local storage and gives you a
unique identifier that can be used to retrieve
that same location later. Use that identifier to
load an anchor, like so. The anchor has a pose
located at the same space in the physical world that it
was at when it was persisted. Android XR doesn't
just give us new ways to show our users things. ARCore for Jetpack XR
introduces capabilities that unlock new ways for your
users to interact with your app, letting you use the
user's hands to create delightful experiences. The primary mode of
interaction on Android XR is with your hands. To obtain detailed hand
tracking and pose information, you'll need to ask for the
user's permission first, using the standard
Android permissions model. Hands are represented by a
collection of 26 joint poses. You can use these poses to
create a variety of interactions using hands. In this example, we
calculate whether or not the index finger is outstretched
by checking whether the index proximal is pointing in the
same direction as the index tip. We can combine
these calculations
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – KbTJNK1ALYc
text:
using hands. In this example, we
calculate whether or not the index finger is outstretched
by checking whether the index proximal is pointing in the
same direction as the index tip. We can combine
these calculations to detect a stop gesture. In this gesture, it's
important that all the fingers are outstretched. In our example app, we use this
to freeze the tiger's animation in its tracks. We're actively adding
new AR features and fixing bugs with
the XR emulator, so be sure to check
out the release notes to see the latest progress
and file bugs or feature requests in the issue tracker. [MUSIC PLAYING] PATRICK FUENTES: We just walked
you through some of the most powerful 3D capabilities
ever available on Android, with real examples. This only scratches the
surface of everything that's happening right
now for Android XR. We've already added support for
the new KHR Animation Pointer specification, and we're in
the process of adding support for the upcoming
KHR Interactivity specification as its emerging. Open standards, like the glTF
specification and its extensions are always evolving,
so we recommend following the Khronos
Group for the latest information on standards. Watch for more announcements
regarding support on Android XR, and look out for which
digital content creation tools are the first to
implement specific extensions. For apps built with Unity,
we've introduced the Android XR SDK for Unity. This SDK builds on Unity's
long-standing support for XR development tools like Ar
Foundation, OpenXR, and the XR Interaction Toolkit, bringing
them together with the platform specific features
tailored for Android XR. With each release,
Unity continues to expand what's
possible on Android XR, adding new capabilities,
improving performance, and enhancing stability
across the stack. Developers can take advantage
of features like URP Application Spacewarp, and
eye-gazed foveated rendering to deliver
high-quality visuals with better performance. Plus XR templates and
a dedicated Android XR
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – KbTJNK1ALYc
text:
Developers can take advantage
of features like URP Application Spacewarp, and
eye-gazed foveated rendering to deliver
high-quality visuals with better performance. Plus XR templates and
a dedicated Android XR build target to
get started faster. Check out the Android XR
blog post to learn more. To help you get started,
we're continuously updating our
documentation, sample code, and the APIs themselves,
as well as our tools. We've added new 3D capabilities
to our Hello Android XR sample app, and we've just recently
added XR differentiated features to the JetStream
sample app, as well. We've also published
a two-part Codelab to help you get started
developing for Android XR. And last but not least,
whenever possible, we recommend that
you position your UI and create panels in Android XR
by using Jetpack Compose for XR. Be sure to check out
our other session, The future is Now with
Compose and AI on Android XR to learn more. This is all a part of
our developer preview, and we genuinely want to hear
from you about what you love, what you wish was
better, and what else we should provide to
help you create immersive, engaging
experiences for your users. Keep the links to the
issue tracker components handy and get started today. The developer community is
one of my favorite things about Android. Dereck and I can't wait to
see what you build in XR. [MUSIC PLAYING]
speaker: unknown
